questions.question: What is an ecosystem? Give two examples of natural and artificial ecosystems.
questions.answer: An ecosystem is a functional unit of the environment consisting of living organisms and their non-living surroundings interacting with each other. Natural: forest, pond; Artificial: aquarium, crop field.

questions.question: Differentiate between food chain and food web.
questions.answer: Food chain is a linear sequence showing transfer of energy from producers to consumers. Food web is a network of interconnected food chains showing multiple feeding relationships in an ecosystem.

questions.question: State the 10% law of energy transfer in an ecosystem.
questions.answer: According to the 10% law, only about 10% of the energy available at one trophic level is transferred to the next trophic level; the rest is lost mainly as heat.

questions.question: Why is the pyramid of energy always upright?
questions.answer: The pyramid of energy is always upright because energy decreases at each successive trophic level due to the 10% law and losses in the form of heat and respiration.

questions.question: What is biomagnification? Give one example.
questions.answer: Biomagnification is the progressive increase in concentration of a non-biodegradable toxic substance at each trophic level in a food chain. Example: DDT accumulation in birds of prey.

questions.question: Name two ozone-depleting substances and state one harmful effect of ozone depletion.
questions.answer: Two ozone-depleting substances are CFCs (chlorofluorocarbons) and halons. Harmful effect: increased UV radiation reaching Earth, causing skin cancer and damage to eyes.

questions.question: What is the role of ozone in the atmosphere?
questions.answer: Ozone in the stratosphere absorbs most of the harmful ultraviolet (UV) radiation from the Sun, protecting living organisms on Earth from DNA damage and skin/eye problems.

questions.question: Differentiate between biodegradable and non-biodegradable substances. Give one example of each.
questions.answer: Biodegradable substances can be broken down by microbes into harmless simpler compounds (example: vegetable peels). Non-biodegradable substances cannot be decomposed by microbes and persist (example: plastic bags).

questions.question: Why should we segregate biodegradable and non-biodegradable wastes before disposal?
questions.answer: Segregation allows biodegradable wastes to be composted easily and non-biodegradable wastes to be recycled or disposed safely, reducing landfill burden, pollution, and promoting resource recovery.

questions.question: Explain the role of decomposers in an ecosystem.
questions.answer: Decomposers break down dead plants and animals into simpler inorganic substances, releasing nutrients back into the soil for reuse by producers, thus completing the nutrient cycle.

questions.question: If 10,000 J of energy is available to producers, how much energy is available to secondary consumers in a food chain? Explain.
questions.answer: Producers: 10,000 J; Primary consumers: 1,000 J; Secondary consumers: 100 J (10% law). Energy decreases at each level due to losses in respiration and heat.

questions.question: Why are most food chains limited to 3–4 trophic levels?
questions.answer: Due to the 10% law, energy available decreases drastically at higher levels (only ~10% transfers), so very little energy remains after 3–4 levels to support another population.

questions.question: How does excessive use of pesticides affect the balance in an ecosystem?
questions.answer: Pesticides kill useful insects and birds, enter food chains, cause biomagnification, reduce biodiversity, and disturb natural predator-prey balance, leading to pest resurgence or ecosystem imbalance.

questions.question: What is the Montreal Protocol? Why is it important?
questions.answer: The Montreal Protocol is an international agreement to phase out production and use of ozone-depleting substances like CFCs. It is important because it has helped slow ozone depletion and allowed partial recovery of the ozone layer.

questions.question: Why do we see fewer top carnivores than herbivores in an ecosystem?
questions.answer: Due to the 10% law, only a small fraction of energy reaches top carnivores, supporting fewer individuals. Herbivores receive more energy directly from producers, allowing larger populations.

questions.question: Explain how UV radiation harms human health if the ozone layer is depleted.
questions.answer: Depleted ozone allows more UV-B rays to reach Earth, causing increased cases of skin cancer, cataracts, weakened immune system, and damage to crops and marine phytoplankton.

questions.question: Why is composting considered a better method for managing biodegradable waste?
questions.answer: Composting converts biodegradable waste into nutrient-rich manure using natural decomposition, reduces landfill use, prevents methane emission, and returns nutrients to soil for plant growth.

questions.question: What will happen if all the decomposers are removed from an ecosystem?
questions.answer: Dead organisms and waste will accumulate, nutrients will not be recycled, soil fertility will decrease, producers will lack raw materials, and the entire food chain will eventually collapse.

questions.question: How does energy flow differ from nutrient cycling in an ecosystem?
questions.answer: Energy flow is unidirectional—from Sun to producers to consumers—and lost as heat. Nutrient cycling is cyclic—nutrients are reused repeatedly through decomposition and uptake by producers.

questions.question: Why are plastics considered harmful to the environment?
questions.answer: Plastics are non-biodegradable, persist for hundreds of years, cause pollution in soil and water, harm wildlife through ingestion/entanglement, and release microplastics that enter food chains.

questions.question: Explain why humans are most affected by biomagnification of harmful chemicals.
questions.answer: Humans occupy the top trophic level, consume organisms from lower levels where toxins have already magnified, and store fat-soluble toxins in body tissues over long periods.

questions.question: What is the significance of the ozone layer being present in the stratosphere?
questions.answer: The stratosphere has high ozone concentration that effectively absorbs UV radiation before it reaches the troposphere and Earth's surface, protecting life from harmful rays.

questions.question: How can students help in reducing non-biodegradable waste in daily life?
questions.answer: Students can carry cloth/jute bags, use metal/steel bottles instead of plastic, segregate waste, reuse notebooks, avoid single-use plastics, and promote recycling in school.

questions.question: Why is it better to use public transport or bicycles instead of private vehicles for short distances?
questions.answer: It reduces fuel consumption, lowers air pollution and greenhouse gases, decreases traffic congestion, conserves fossil fuels, and helps maintain ecological balance.

questions.question: Explain how excessive use of chemical fertilizers affects the environment.
questions.answer: Excess fertilizers run off into water bodies causing eutrophication (algal blooms), oxygen depletion, death of aquatic life, and soil degradation due to nutrient imbalance and reduced fertility over time.
